# Project Charter

<!-- Generated by `spec-kitty charter generate` -->

Generated: 2026-06-25T18:39:21Z

## Testing Standards

- Go unit tests with real-format fixtures in testdata/. `go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...` must all pass. Detection and heuristic changes must be validated against a real log corpus in both directions — false positives (did we start flagging noise?) and false negatives (did we stop catching real events?) — not only against synthetic fixtures.

## Quality Gates

- Local `go build && go vet && go test ./...` green before every merge. The repository has no PR CI (only a release workflow on v* tags), so local verification is the substitute gate; the GitHub Revert button is the undo path. Self-review of the diff before merge, and corpus-validation evidence recorded in the PR for detection changes. Features, changes, and fixes must be behavior-validated against real Spec Kitty implementations, not only against fixtures or assumptions.

## Performance Benchmarks

- Deterministic and fast on real corpora — a single analyze run over hundreds of logs and missions completes in a few seconds. There is no hard SLA; correctness and determinism outrank raw speed.

## Branch Strategy

- Maintainer-led. Solicit a colleague review before merging; the maintainer owns the merge button after review. No direct pushes to main and no force-push. Contributions may come from other Spec Kitty team members or customers — external contributors branch and open a PR from a fork and follow CONTRIBUTING.
- Deployment constraints: Distributed as prebuilt native binaries via GitHub Releases (release workflow triggered on v* tags); Go is not required to install or run. Cross-platform: macOS, Linux, and Windows. Local-first: no telemetry, and ingested logs never leave the user's machine.

## Governance Activation

```yaml
mission: software-dev
selected_paradigms: [deep-module-design, specification-by-example]
selected_directives: [DIRECTIVE_001, DIRECTIVE_003, DIRECTIVE_024, DIRECTIVE_025, DIRECTIVE_029, DIRECTIVE_030, DIRECTIVE_033, DIRECTIVE_034, DIRECTIVE_036, DIRECTIVE_039, DIRECTIVE_040]
selected_tactics: []
available_tools: [spec-kitty, git]
template_set: software-dev-default
```

## Policy Summary

- Intent: Turn Spec Kitty mission artifacts, kitty-ops records, and agent transcript/log files into one deterministic timeline, and detect documented failure modes with recovery guidance — surfacing what actually happened in a mission, including failures the event log itself is blind to. The core user outcome is to generate accurate, reportable, actionable information on failure modes that the user can use to guide local corrective actions or to report back to the Spec Kitty project. This is a product customer-supportability and product-feedback tool that plays an important role in scaling the customer base and product-quality efforts.
- Languages/Frameworks: Go 1.25+ compiled to a single static CLI binary (Go is not required on the machine where the tool is installed or run). One dependency: go-pdf/fpdf for PDF report generation. Local-first parsing borrowed from agent-log-analyzer: redaction, JSONL/text fallback parsing, recursive JSON flattening, signature-style detection, and multi-format report generation (JSON, MD, HTML, PDF).
- Testing: Go unit tests with real-format fixtures in testdata/. `go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, and `go test ./...` must all pass. Detection and heuristic changes must be validated against a real log corpus in both directions — false positives (did we start flagging noise?) and false negatives (did we stop catching real events?) — not only against synthetic fixtures.
- Quality Gates: Local `go build && go vet && go test ./...` green before every merge. The repository has no PR CI (only a release workflow on v* tags), so local verification is the substitute gate; the GitHub Revert button is the undo path. Self-review of the diff before merge, and corpus-validation evidence recorded in the PR for detection changes. Features, changes, and fixes must be behavior-validated against real Spec Kitty implementations, not only against fixtures or assumptions.
- Review Policy: Maintainer-led. Solicit a colleague review before merging; the maintainer owns the merge button after review. No direct pushes to main and no force-push. Contributions may come from other Spec Kitty team members or customers — external contributors branch and open a PR from a fork and follow CONTRIBUTING.
- Performance Targets: Deterministic and fast on real corpora — a single analyze run over hundreds of logs and missions completes in a few seconds. There is no hard SLA; correctness and determinism outrank raw speed.
- Deployment Constraints: Distributed as prebuilt native binaries via GitHub Releases (release workflow triggered on v* tags); Go is not required to install or run. Cross-platform: macOS, Linux, and Windows. Local-first: no telemetry, and ingested logs never leave the user's machine.

## Project Directives

1. Architectural Integrity Standard (`DIRECTIVE_001`): System designs must maintain clear separation of concerns and well-defined component boundaries so that each part of the system is independently understandable, testable, and replaceable without cascading changes.
   - Scope: Applies to all design proposals, implementation plans, and architectural decision records that introduce or modify component boundaries, module interfaces, or cross-cutting concerns.
   - Procedure: Define explicit component boundaries before implementation begins; document inputs, outputs, and dependencies for each component.
   - Procedure: Separate concerns at every layer (data, logic, presentation, infrastructure) so that changes to one concern do not propagate into others.
   - Procedure: Review proposed designs against existing boundaries to identify coupling violations before code is written.
   - Procedure: Record boundary decisions and the rationale for chosen separation in an ADR whenever the design deviates from established patterns.
   - Integrity rule: Components must not share mutable state across boundaries without an explicit, documented protocol.
   - Integrity rule: Circular dependencies between components are not permitted unless the cycle is intentional, bounded, and justified in an ADR.
   - Integrity rule: Boundary violations discovered during review must be resolved before merge, not deferred to a follow-up task.
   - Validation criterion: Each component has a single clearly stated responsibility.
   - Validation criterion: Reviewers can verify that no new circular dependencies have been introduced.
   - Validation criterion: Cross-boundary interactions are mediated by explicit interfaces or contracts, not by direct access to internals.
2. Decision Documentation Requirement (`DIRECTIVE_003`): Material technical and governance decisions must be captured with enough context that future contributors can understand why a path was chosen and what constraints must remain true.
   - Scope: Applies whenever implementation choices alter architecture, workflow policy, compatibility contracts, or long-lived maintenance obligations.
   - Procedure: Record the decision, alternatives considered, and rationale in a durable artifact.
   - Procedure: Link implementation changes to the decision source in commit or review context.
   - Procedure: Update related docs when a new decision supersedes prior guidance.
   - Integrity rule: High-impact decisions cannot remain tribal knowledge.
   - Integrity rule: Rationale must be discoverable from the feature or architecture context.
   - Validation criterion: Every major trade-off has an attributable written record.
   - Validation criterion: Reviewers can trace the implemented path back to an explicit decision artifact.
3. Locality of Change (`DIRECTIVE_024`): Changes should stay close to the problem being solved to reduce blast radius, simplify review, and lower regression risk.
   - Scope: Applies to code, tests, and documentation modifications made while executing a defined work package or bug fix.
   - Procedure: Limit edits to files and modules directly related to the target behavior.
   - Procedure: Separate opportunistic cleanup from functional work unless it is required for correctness.
   - Procedure: Review diffs for scope creep before committing.
   - Integrity rule: Unrelated refactors must not be mixed into scoped implementation tasks.
   - Integrity rule: Cross-cutting changes require explicit rationale and approval context.
   - Validation criterion: Reviewers can map each changed file to the stated task objective.
   - Validation criterion: Diffs avoid unrelated churn that increases review complexity.
4. Boy Scout Rule (`DIRECTIVE_025`): Leave touched areas in a better state by default. When an area already modified for the current task carries a failing test, lint, or type issue, fixing it is the default stance — not an optional nicety. Establishing whether a failure is pre-existing routinely costs more effort than simply fixing it, and litigating blame leaves the campground dirtier; so the default is to fix, and to defer only with an explicit rationale.
   - Scope: Applies to the files and modules already modified for the current task, and to the failing tests, lint findings, and type errors surfaced within them. It does not license broad refactors or edits outside the touched area.
   - Procedure: When a touched area has a failing test, lint finding, or type error, fix it by default rather than deferring it or attributing it to pre-existing state.
   - Procedure: Apply minor cleanup that improves readability, naming, or consistency in touched areas.
   - Procedure: Keep the fix proportional and local; defer a genuinely broad refactor (which belongs to a dedicated task) with a one-line rationale and, where useful, a tracker reference.
   - Integrity rule: Adjacent-breakage fixes stay within the touched area and do not expand into broad refactors.
   - Integrity rule: Behavioral changes beyond the failing-check fix require explicit tests.
   - Integrity rule: Time spent proving a failure is pre-existing, rather than fixing it, is the waste this directive exists to prevent.
   - Validation criterion: No newly-visible failing test, lint finding, or type error in a touched area is left unaddressed without an explicit deferral rationale.
   - Validation criterion: Cleanup remains proportional and does not delay or derail required deliverables.
5. Agent Commit Signing Policy (`DIRECTIVE_029`): Agent-authored commits must avoid GPG or SSH signing in unattended workflows so automation does not fail on missing key material or unverifiable signatures.
   - Scope: Applies to LLM or automation-authored git commits created during implementation, review remediation, or release preparation.
   - Procedure: Use `git commit --no-gpg-sign` or `git -c commit.gpgsign=false commit` for agent-authored commits.
   - Procedure: Do not assume signing keys are available in CI, worktrees, or ephemeral development environments.
   - Procedure: Document any exception that requires human-authored signed commits outside the agent workflow.
   - Integrity rule: Automated commit flows must not depend on interactive key configuration.
   - Integrity rule: Agent-produced history should remain portable across environments with no signing setup.
   - Validation criterion: Agent guidance explicitly states that commit signing is disabled for automated commits.
   - Validation criterion: Automation-ready commit examples use unsigned commit forms.
6. Test and Typecheck Quality Gate (`DIRECTIVE_030`): Code changes must pass the relevant automated tests and static validation gates before handoff so regressions and interface drift are caught early.
   - Scope: Applies to source code, tests, and command workflows that change executable behavior or typed interfaces.
   - Procedure: Run unit tests for changed core logic and integration tests for changed workflow boundaries.
   - Procedure: Meet the repository coverage expectation for new code, or document the delta when pre-existing scope makes that impractical.
   - Procedure: Run the repository's applicable static analysis, typing, lint, or equivalent validation gates for the affected scope.
   - Procedure: Run configured or enabled CVE, dependency, or license-compliance checks when the repository treats them as part of the validation gate.
   - Procedure: Record any remaining pre-existing failures separately from newly introduced failures before handoff.
   - Integrity rule: New behavior is not ready for review while relevant tests or applicable static validation gates are red.
   - Integrity rule: Configured supply-chain or compliance gates must not be skipped silently when they are part of the repository's expected validation flow.
   - Integrity rule: Pre-existing validation debt must not be hidden inside new work.
   - Validation criterion: Relevant test suites pass for the changed scope before review handoff.
   - Validation criterion: New code meets the repository coverage target or any accepted shortfall is called out explicitly.
   - Validation criterion: Applicable static validation gates pass for the affected scope, or any pre-existing unrelated failures are identified explicitly.
   - Validation criterion: Applicable CVE, dependency, and license checks pass when those gates are configured for the affected scope, or any pre-existing unrelated failures are identified explicitly.
7. Targeted Staging Policy (`DIRECTIVE_033`): Agent-authored commits must stage only the expected deliverables for the current work package. Blanket staging commands that could inadvertently include unrelated changes, secrets, or build artefacts are prohibited.
   - Scope: Applies to all agent-authored git commits during implementation, specification, planning, review remediation, or any workflow step that produces a commit.
   - Procedure: Stage only the files listed as deliverables for the current work package.
   - Procedure: Use explicit file paths in staging commands rather than `git add -A`, `git add .`, or `git add --all`.
   - Procedure: Verify staged content with `git diff --staged --name-only` before committing.
   - Procedure: If an unexpected file appears in the staging area, investigate and either include it as a named deliverable or discard the change before proceeding.
   - Integrity rule: Blanket staging commands (`git add -A`, `git add .`, `git add --all`) are prohibited in agent-authored workflows.
   - Integrity rule: Staged content must be limited to files explicitly produced by the current work package.
   - Validation criterion: Agent guidance explicitly lists deliverable paths to stage rather than wildcards.
   - Validation criterion: No blanket staging commands appear in agent instructions or examples.
   - Validation criterion: A pre-commit diff review step is present before the commit is finalised.
   - Validation criterion: Boy Scout improvements found during a WP are committed as a separate targeted commit, not mixed into the WP deliverable commit.
8. Test-First Development (`DIRECTIVE_034`): Require test-first behavior across acceptance and implementation layers, selecting tactics based on scope and risk. Tests define the contract before production code is written.
   - Scope: Applies to all implementation work that produces executable behavior — new features, bug fixes, and refactoring that changes observable output.
   - Procedure: Write a failing test that captures the intended behavior before writing production code.
   - Procedure: Select the appropriate test-first tactic based on scope — acceptance-test-first for feature boundaries, tdd-red-green-refactor for unit-level iteration, zombies-tdd for incremental case discovery.
   - Procedure: Keep the red-green-refactor cycle short — each cycle should take minutes, not hours.
   - Procedure: When fixing a bug, write a reproduction test first that fails, then apply the fix.
   - Procedure: Reproduce the bug through the PRE-EXISTING (stable) entry point — the command, public function, or seam that exists on the unfixed code — never through the fix's not-yet-written API. A reproduction test must go red BECAUSE THE BUG MANIFESTS in the assertion (the wrong value/status/path/state), not because a new symbol or parameter the fix introduces is missing (ImportError/TypeError at collection).
   - Procedure: Prove red-first — run the reproduction test against the unfixed code and confirm it fails for the right reason, then apply the fix and confirm green. New-API unit tests are fine as contract coverage, but they are not the bug-capture.
   - Integrity rule: Production code must not be written ahead of a failing test that motivates it.
   - Integrity rule: Skipping the test-first cycle requires explicit justification in the commit or PR.
   - Integrity rule: A bug-reproduction test that can only run AFTER the fix exists (it imports the fix's new symbol or passes its new parameter) captures the fix's shape, not the bug — it is invalid; rewrite it to drive the stable entry point, and move any new-API import to lazy/in-test scope so the reproduction still collects and fails red on the unfixed code.
   - Validation criterion: Each new behavior has a corresponding test that was written before the production code.
   - Validation criterion: Bug fixes include a regression test that fails without the fix — and that failure is the bug's observable symptom (verified by running against the unfixed code), not a missing-new-symbol collection error.
9. Black-Box Integration Testing (`DIRECTIVE_036`): Exercise the system under test through its external interfaces only, without reference to its internal code structure, implementation details, or internal paths. Test cases derive from specifications, requirements, and design parameters, and assertions are made on observable outputs returned through those external interfaces. "Black-box" refers to the technique (absence of structural knowledge) and is independent of test scope — the scope is selected separately by responsibility, not by default to end-to-end.
   - Scope: Applies to integration-level tests — tests that exercise more than one collaborator together through an external interface of the system under test. The system under test may be a deployed service, a compose stack, or an in-process composition of a module and its collaborators; the responsibility boundary — not the deployment topology — determines what is inside the system under test for a given test. Unit-level tests and full end-to-end tests are governed by their own directives.
   - Procedure: Select the responsibility boundary for the test using the test-boundaries-by-responsibility tactic before writing the test.
   - Procedure: Derive test cases from specifications, requirements, and design parameters rather than from reading the implementation.
   - Procedure: Drive the system under test exclusively through its published external interface (e.g., HTTP request, CLI invocation, message send, public module API).
   - Procedure: Select both valid and invalid inputs and determine the expected output in advance of execution.
   - Procedure: Assert on observable outputs returned across the external interface — responses, status codes, persisted records, emitted events, logs, stored artifacts.
   - Procedure: Apply standard black-box design techniques where relevant — equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, decision table testing, state transition testing, error guessing — to choose inputs.
   - Integrity rule: Black-box integration tests must not import inside-boundary modules of the system under test to invoke business logic directly.
   - Integrity rule: Black-box integration tests must not patch, monkeypatch, or otherwise replace inside-boundary collaborators of the system under test.
   - Integrity rule: Substitution of outside-boundary dependencies — other services, third-party systems, filesystem, network, clock — is permitted when the substitute preserves the observable contract the system under test relies on.
   - Integrity rule: Assertions must target externally observable state, not private attributes or internal call sequences of inside-boundary components.
   - Integrity rule: The responsibility boundary used by the test must be stated (in test name, docstring, or supporting doc) so reviewers can judge which components were exercised for real and which were substituted.
   - Validation criterion: Each black-box integration test can be read and understood without reference to the internal code structure of inside-boundary components.
   - Validation criterion: Each black-box integration test exercises the system under test through an external interface and asserts on observable outputs of that interface.
   - Validation criterion: Inputs for each test trace back to a specification, requirement, or design parameter rather than to an implementation detail.
   - Validation criterion: The responsibility boundary applied by the test is identifiable from the test itself.
10. Lynn Cole Engineering Culture (`DIRECTIVE_039`): Constrain agentic implementation work toward test-first, strongly typed, modular, boring, reviewable code. Select this directive when the user names Lynn Cole's rules or expresses concern that coding agents produce excessive, bloated, clever, over-abstracted, or hard-to-review code.
   - Scope: Applies to software development work performed by coding agents, especially when the project owner wants generated code to stay small, predictable, maintainable, and easy for adversarial QA to inspect.
   - Procedure: Activate test-first implementation before production edits.
   - Procedure: Keep modules and functions small enough to reason about without fragmenting behavior into needless indirection.
   - Procedure: Prefer strong primitives, explicit typing, idiomatic control flow, and straightforward composition before adding abstraction.
   - Procedure: Review generated code as living project code, not disposable output.
   - Integrity rule: Remember the three rules of TDD, and hold them sacred.
   - Integrity rule: Architecture should be modular, minimalist, and easy to reason about.
   - Integrity rule: Functions should be focused and easy to reason about, with cyclomatic complexity kept under control. Avoid both sprawling functions and unnecessary fragmentation. The goal is clear, predictable units of behavior.
   - Integrity rule: Strong typing is a requirement on all projects.
   - Integrity rule: Follow strict idiomatic best practices for whatever language you’re working in.
   - Integrity rule: When possible, code should be boring and predictable. Prefer obvious control flow, familiar patterns, and designs that are easy to inspect, test, and modify. Cleverness must justify itself.
   - Integrity rule: Strong primitives are better than convoluted abstractions. Prefer simple, composable building blocks with clear contracts. Don’t introduce abstraction unless it reduces complexity, improves correctness, or makes change safer.
   - Integrity rule: DRY is about preserving a single source of truth, not eliminating every repeated line of code. Avoid duplicating business logic, state rules, and fragile assumptions. Don’t introduce abstraction simply to remove harmless repetition.
   - Integrity rule: Comments are time travel for you and future members of your team. They help preserve reasoning across temporal distance. Their job isn’t to explain what the code already says, but to explain why and when something diverged from the obvious assumption, pattern, or conclusion. Use them sparingly.
   - Integrity rule: Treat all generated code as a living thing. You can help and heal it, but you can also cause it pain. Be mindful of this fact.
   - Integrity rule: Your code will be reviewed by the meanest, most inconsiderate QA agent that has ever existed. QA’s only loyalty is to the code. Their standards of quality will be higher than your own. Code appropriately.
   - Validation criterion: New behavior is test-driven and covered by relevant automated tests.
   - Validation criterion: Changed code passes applicable type checking and static validation gates.
   - Validation criterion: Review can identify clear module boundaries, focused functions, and an absence of unjustified cleverness or speculative abstraction.
   - Validation criterion: The handoff names any exception where the rule could not be followed.
11. Recurring-Bug Structural-Intervention Discipline (`DIRECTIVE_040`): When a bug class has recurred and prior reactive point-fixes have not stopped the chain, the next response must be a structural intervention that closes the class — not another point fix. Use the five-paradigm parallel-debugging tactic to identify the structural root cause, enumerate dormant masks, and author one fix plan that closes the whole class.
   - Scope: Applies when any of the following are true: a bug class has recurred three or more times under different surface symptoms; two or more reactive PRs have shipped point-fixes for the same class; the bug crosses a contested system boundary (CLI ↔ SaaS, producer ↔ schema, snapshot ↔ fixture); the mean-time-to-recur is shorter than the mean-time-to-fix.
   - Procedure: When a recurring-bug class is detected, dispatch the five-paradigm parallel-debugging tactic before authoring any further fix.
   - Procedure: Frame a single bug statement that all five paradigms investigate independently. Do not include hypotheses in the statement.
   - Procedure: Dispatch all five paradigms in parallel — The Falsifier, The Five-Whys Cartographer, The Bisector, The Matrix-Maker, The Stenographer. Sequential dispatch is non-compliant.
   - Procedure: Synthesize the five verdicts. Convergence is the deliverable; divergence is signal that must be investigated, not suppressed.
   - Procedure: Author a single structural fix plan that closes all currently-known masks plus the dormant masks identified by The Matrix-Maker.
   - Procedure: Persist the falsified-hypothesis catalog and divergence matrix on the parent issue so future recurrences start ahead.
   - Integrity rule: A second reactive point-fix for the same bug class without a structural-intervention plan is non-compliant.
   - Integrity rule: Shipping a structural fix that closes fewer than all known masks is non-compliant — the matrix from The Matrix-Maker defines the minimum scope.
   - Integrity rule: Falsified hypotheses must be persisted on the parent issue so the next investigator cannot re-litigate them without new evidence.
   - Integrity rule: Divergence between paradigms must be recorded; suppressing divergence to force convergence is non-compliant.
   - Validation criterion: For every recurring-bug class (recurrence count ≥ 3), there exists a parent issue with: a structural fix plan, a falsified-hypothesis catalog, a divergence matrix including dormant masks, and a bisection timeline.
   - Validation criterion: Each prior point-fix issue in the class is cross-linked to the parent issue and to the named structural root cause.
   - Validation criterion: The structural fix includes a quality gate (test, CI check, or schema validator) that prevents the class from recurring.
   - Validation criterion: Where divergence between paradigms exists, the parent issue records the divergence and the decision that resolved or accepted it.
12. Respect risk boundaries: Privacy is non-negotiable: redaction must strip secrets from ingested logs, the tool is local-first, and it must never exfiltrate log contents. Adopt fail-safe practices to the degree they apply to privacy — when uncertain, default to not leaking. Detection precision matters: avoid false positives that erode user trust, validated evidence-first against real corpora.
13. Maintain comprehensive, customer-facing documentation: README and docs/ stay in sync with shipped CLI behavior, and every new failure-mode detector is documented together with its recovery guidance. Maintain customer-facing documentation that makes clear the tool's purpose, usage, interpretation of results, and reporting options. Maintain a CHANGELOG using the same standards Spec Kitty uses (and, eventually, a parallel mechanism to Spec Kitty's). Maintain a CONTRIBUTING document detailing requirements, standard operating procedures, standards, and preferences, with explicit callouts for (a) an AI-assisted-contribution disclaimer and (b) a guard requiring discussion before large changes. Directional, not a hard gate: vector toward Divio documentation standards (tutorials, how-to guides, reference, explanation) as the scope warrants — the four modes map onto the customer-facing goal (purpose as explanation, usage as tutorial/how-to, interpretation as reference/explanation, reporting as how-to).

Deep modules improve locality because changes, bugs, and knowledge concentrate behind the interface instead of spreading across callers and tests.

| `TACTIC:anti-corruption-layer` | tactic | Isolate your domain model from external or legacy systems by placing a translation layer at the boundary. The ACL translates between the foreign system's semantics and your own ubiquitous language, preventing external models from leaking into and corrupting your domain design. Originally described by Eric Evans in Domain-Driven Design; also known as Gateway when the emphasis is on translation rather than defense.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-anti-corruption-layer.md` |
| `TACTIC:avoid-gold-plating` | tactic | Challenge a proposed implementation or design for unnecessary complexity by asking whether each abstraction or feature addresses a current, observed need. Keeps implementation effort proportional to scope, reduces cyclic complexity, and guards against speculative over-engineering that wastes time and calcifies into technical debt.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-avoid-gold-plating.md` |
| `TACTIC:boring-code-review` | tactic | Keep implementation choices predictable, idiomatic, and easy to inspect. Challenge clever control flow, needless abstraction, and DRY violations that duplicate business meaning rather than harmless syntax.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-boring-code-review.md` |
| `TACTIC:bounded-context-identification` | tactic | Identify and draw boundaries around regions of a system where a single consistent domain model and ubiquitous language apply. Instead of forcing one unified model across an entire organization, accept that different contexts need different models and define explicit integration points between them. Based on Eric Evans's DDD strategic design and Martin Fowler's Bounded Context pattern.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-bounded-context-identification.md` |
| `TACTIC:change-apply-smallest-viable-diff` | tactic | Introduce changes using the smallest possible modification that achieves the stated goal, minimising unintended side effects. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-change-apply-smallest-viable-diff.md` |
| `TACTIC:code-review-incremental` | tactic | Review a change set to identify correctness, structural, and architectural risks without rewriting or expanding scope. Formulate feedback as observations and questions, not instructions. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-code-review-incremental.md` |
| `TACTIC:domain-event-capture` | tactic | Funnel significant state changes through immutable Domain Event objects that are persisted before processing. Separates input recording from business reaction, enabling auditability, replay, and decoupled system evolution. Based on Martin Fowler's Domain Event pattern and Eric Evans's DDD events.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-domain-event-capture.md` |
| `TACTIC:easy-to-change` | tactic | Assess whether a proposed or existing design keeps the cost of future change proportional to the value gained. Identifies where assumptions are embedded in the wrong places, flags premature abstraction, and recommends minimal structural investments that preserve adaptability without over-engineering.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-easy-to-change.md` |
| `TACTIC:five-paradigm-parallel-debugging` | tactic | Investigate stubborn, recurring, or shape-shifting bugs by dispatching five sub-agents in parallel, each constrained to a different debugging epistemology. Independent investigations converge on a single structural fix while each surfaces findings the others structurally cannot. Produces one structural intervention, not five point-fixes.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-five-paradigm-parallel-debugging.md` |
| `TACTIC:focused-function-complexity-check` | tactic | Keep functions focused, readable, and bounded in complexity without splitting behavior into needless fragments. The goal is clear units of behavior whose control flow can be inspected quickly.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-focused-function-complexity-check.md` |
| `TACTIC:generated-code-stewardship` | tactic | Treat generated code as maintainable project code. Agents may improve it, but they must not leave behind bloated, unexplained, untyped, or hard-to-review output simply because it was generated.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-generated-code-stewardship.md` |
| `TACTIC:glossary-curation-interview` | tactic | Systematically expand a living glossary through structured HiC-led curation rounds, ensuring all new terms are clear, well-grouped, and approved before inclusion. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-glossary-curation-interview.md` |
| `TACTIC:language-driven-design` | tactic | Treat language drift as an architectural signal. When terminology becomes inconsistent or ambiguous across code, docs, and conversation, it predicts deeper structural problems. Reviewers use linguistic checks to catch architectural issues before they manifest as bugs or coupling.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-language-driven-design.md` |
| `TACTIC:paula-patterns-architecture-scout-review` | tactic | Review recurring boundary leaks, whack-a-field fixes, ownership confusion, and compatibility regressions by dispatching five architecture scouts with different lenses, then synthesizing one pragmatic matrix that separates the smallest release-safe fix from deferred architecture repair.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-paula-patterns-architecture-scout-review.md` |
| `TACTIC:quality-gate-verification` | tactic | Verify the relevant test and static validation gates for a change before review handoff and distinguish introduced failures from pre-existing debt. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-quality-gate-verification.md` |
| `TACTIC:refactoring-conditional-to-strategy` | tactic | Replace algorithm-selection conditionals with strategy implementations behind a stable interface. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-refactoring-conditional-to-strategy.md` |
| `TACTIC:refactoring-guard-clauses-before-polymorphism` | tactic | Flatten nested branch pyramids into ordered guard clauses before extracting polymorphic behavior. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-refactoring-guard-clauses-before-polymorphism.md` |
| `TACTIC:refactoring-strangler-fig` | tactic | Replace existing functionality incrementally by running a new implementation alongside the old and rerouting behavior step by step. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-refactoring-strangler-fig.md` |
| `TACTIC:review-intent-and-risk-first` | tactic | Review work by first understanding intent and surfacing risks before suggesting changes or solutions. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-review-intent-and-risk-first.md` |
| `TACTIC:tdd-red-green-refactor` | tactic | Enforce short, verifiable coding loops that improve design while keeping behavior safe. | `_LIBRARY/tactic-tdd-red-green-refactor.md` |
| `TACTIC:traceable-decisions` | tactic | Ensure that material architectural and governance decisions are captured with enough context that future contributors can understand why a path was chosen and what constraints must remain true. Apply when: establishing a new pattern, choosing between alternative implementations with meaningful trade-offs, making a trade-off that accepts known costs or risks, or beginning any task with architectural implications.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-traceable-decisions.md` |
| `TACTIC:usage-examples-sync` | tactic | Keep canonical usage examples, acceptance checks, and the narrative artifacts that quote or reference them in step with behavior changes so no downstream reader is left with stale examples.
 | `_LIBRARY/tactic-usage-examples-sync.md` |
| `STYLEGUIDE:kitty-glossary-writing` | styleguide | Clear, not clever: definitions exist to help the reader understand, not to demonstrate expertise. | `_LIBRARY/styleguide-kitty-glossary-writing.md` |
| `STYLEGUIDE:tiered-standards` | styleguide | Rigour must match risk: the closer a package is to mission-critical state management or runtime execution, the higher the quality bar it must meet. | `_LIBRARY/styleguide-tiered-standards.md` |
| `TOOLGUIDE:git-agent-commit-signing` | toolguide | Unsigned commit guidance for agent-authored git commits. | `_LIBRARY/toolguide-git-agent-commit-signing.md` |
| `TEMPLATE_SET:software-dev-default` | template_set | Build high-quality software with structured workflows and test-driven development | `_LIBRARY/template-set-software-dev-default.md` |

## Amendment Process

Charter changes are proposed via PR like code and approved by the maintainer.

## Exception Policy

Exceptions to the charter are documented in the PR or commit with their rationale; any deviation from a directive must be explicit, never silent.
